interactions.summary: Jiang frames the second game-theory class as a training exercise in seeing everyday life as a game. School officially exists to create functional, creative people through literacy, core competencies, and lifelong learning, but most schools fail so badly that they make students hate reading, collaboration, thinking, and learning.

He turns to his own 2008 school-reform story: after studying at Yale he was hired to build an international program in South China, found a test-and-memorization culture, and introduced seminars, books, a large English library, and practical student-run activities.

Jiang describes the student coffee house as a real business and collaboration exercise. A student asks whether it was like the current campus coffee store, and Jiang answers that in 2008 his was the first such school activity and that it still exists years later.
interactions.speaker_attribution: Student question followed by Jiang answer.

interactions.summary: He adds the daily newspaper, then explains the cultural system behind the reform: transparency, innovation, and openness, with a curriculum that could admit mistakes and evolve. The program became highly successful by admissions and reputation measures, yet he was fired and treated as a dictator or asshole.

Jiang identifies the game-theory mistake: he insisted on fairness, but the game is established by stakeholders pursuing interests. He names the players and begins contrasting his idealized 2008 assumptions with their actual incentives.

Jiang contrasts his naive 2008 assumptions about students, parents, teachers, administrators, government, and colleges with the harsher game-theory rule: players seek the best possible result with the least possible work. He then gives each player a self-interested motive.

While listing players and ranking power, a student reminds him about the government. Jiang accepts the correction, adds government back in, and then ranks the game: parents and teachers matter most, while students, government, and colleges matter far less than their public rhetoric implies.
interactions.speaker_attribution: Jiang lecture with brief student correction.

interactions.summary: He starts the player-by-player analysis by saying students mainly seek popularity, friendship, parental and teacher approval, grades, fun, and college, not learning itself.

Jiang analyzes parents: they want successful children, but success functions as face. Ivy League names and expensive international schools become luxury signals, with white teachers as the key marketing proof rather than educational quality.

He then assigns incentives to teachers, administrators, government, and colleges: teachers minimize work, administrators protect parent relationships, government wants no problems and compliant future citizens, and American colleges want paying students.
A student asks what transformed education from its proper purposes into schools that do not fulfill them. Jiang begins answering by returning to superstructure and the lifecycle of societies.
interactions.speaker_attribution: Student question followed by Jiang answer beginning.

interactions.summary: Answering the student's transformation question, Jiang defines the health of a society through energy, openness, and cohesion. When all three are strong, schools invest in the future, respect teachers, and can be excellent, with Finland and earlier China as examples.

He then describes decline: wealth, inequality, and corruption weaken cohesion into individual zero-sum competition, punish openness by making administrators afraid to admit mistakes, and drain energy as students and teachers lose motivation.

Jiang returns from superstructure to the school-game list: parents want white faces and grades, teachers want to get by, administrators protect parent relationships, and the convergence produces today's international-school surface.

After summarizing slow incremental reform, Jiang asks for questions. A student asks whether they will learn how to find what causes different stakeholders to have different interests. Jiang reframes the question as where player interests come from and answers with superstructure, culture, and the fact that each person is also playing other games.
interactions.speaker_attribution: Student question followed by Jiang answer.

interactions.summary: Jiang explains parents through overlapping identities. As family members, they compete with siblings through money, children, spouse, and lifestyle. As colleagues, they must keep their children aligned with the colleague network or risk social exile.

He generalizes the answer: players compete and cooperate at the same time because they fear being thrown out of the game. His own reform violated norms and exposed followers to ostracism. Students also play multiple games, and learning usually matters less than pleasing parents, having friends, and adapting to whichever game is rewarded.
Jiang closes the class after checking for more questions and says they will continue next class.

speaker_notes.note: SPEAKER_01 is likely a student asking about a local coffee-store comparison.
The short 'The government?' exchange is a student correction embedded inside Jiang's segment, despite the same diarization label.
SPEAKER_00 is a student asking a substantive question about the cause of educational decline.
SPEAKER_00 is a student asking a real question about the source of stakeholder interests.

claims.claim: Jiang defines the game-theory course goal as learning to analyze why events happen and predict how they develop by seeing the motivations behind behavior.
He says school is supposed to train students for social function and creativity through literacy, core competencies, and lifelong learning.

He argues that most schools do not merely fail to teach these purposes; they produce the opposite effect by weakening reading, turning collaboration into zero-sum competition, and making students hate learning.

In Jiang's account, the 2008 South China program began from replacing memorization and performative activities with seminar discussion, real reading, student-run business practice, and doing rather than merely talking.

Jiang says the coffee house worked because students ran a real business, served customers, collaborated, and learned finance and entrepreneurship rather than merely performing an extracurricular label.
He says the daily newspaper required students to gather information, write, edit, publish, and deliver under real deadlines, making it a demanding practice environment.
He presents transparency, innovation, and openness as the cultural principles needed for a reforming curriculum to learn from mistakes and evolve.
Jiang says the program succeeded by conventional standards, including student outcomes and regional reputation, but the same stakeholders still pushed him out.
claims.temporal_scope: Autobiographical account of the post-2008 reform period.

claims.claim: His core explanation is that fairness was not how the school game was played; the game was made by stakeholders who played according to their interests.

Jiang's game-theory principle here is that players seek the best possible result for the least possible work; he names this as the simple core behind student, parent, teacher, administrator, and government behavior.
He says a game is constructed when players agree on its rules and incentives; an outsider who tries to build a different game loses legitimacy when players feel control slipping away.
He argues that game analysis must rank player power because not all players matter equally; in schools, students may be the majority yet their actual wants matter little.

In Jiang's ranking, parents matter because they pay and can create trouble; teachers matter because they implement the rules; government and colleges often care only that the school produces no problems or paying students.
He says students are in school mainly to please parents and teachers because parents pay for life and teachers give grades; learning is not the actual priority in the game.
Jiang argues that many parents treat education as a status good: Ivy League names and expensive international schools matter because they provide face before relatives, friends, and colleagues.
He says white teachers are the main marketing tool of international schools because parents read 'white faces' as proof of quality regardless of curriculum, teacher competence, or learning.
He portrays teachers and administrators as workers trying to get by: teachers minimize effort, while administrators sell the school by giving parents what they want.
He says the government's practical demand is no problems and compliance, so a school that trains obedience can count as a good school from the state's point of view.
He claims American colleges will take international students primarily because they can pay, even if the admissions rhetoric says they seek passion and curiosity.
When asked what caused the transformation, Jiang answers by invoking superstructure, defined as the macro picture of society: demographics, economy, politics, and religion.
Jiang uses three metrics for societal development: energy, openness, and cohesion.
He argues that excellent schools arise when a society is energetic, open, and cohesive enough to invest in every child's future and make teaching high-status, autonomous work.

He says Chinese schools in the 1980s were better in part because teachers were respected, felt responsibility for the young, took joy in their work, and students faced less pressure while learning more.

He diagnoses school decline as a superstructural effect of wealth generation, inequality, and corruption: cohesion turns into individualization, openness is punished, and energy collapses into hiding and minimal effort.
He says that in 2025-2026, some parents' wealth weakens student motivation because school success is no longer the only route to material security.
When the player interests converge in Chinese international schools, Jiang says the result is white faces, easy grades, scholarships for a few top students, cheating, and high turnover.

Jiang says player interests come from the superstructure of society and culture, but game theory also has to see that each player is simultaneously playing several other games through several identities.

He models parents as people with multiple identities: in the family game they compete with siblings by producing visible success, while in the colleague game they seek social acceptance through children who fit the colleague network.
He says social players fear ostracism or exile from the group, so they try to beat others while also avoiding behavior that gets them thrown out of the game.
He interprets backlash against his reform as a defense of norms: by arguing against local conventions, he made supporters risk ostracism by the larger community.

He says students also play multiple games: doing well in school, friendship/popularity, and pleasing parents; in his ranking, pleasing parents comes first, friendship second, and actual learning last.
He defines game theory here as a descriptive account of how players behave in response to incentives and the game they believe they are playing, not as ideals or how things should be.
The lecture ends as a continuing sequence rather than a completed topic; Jiang explicitly says the class will continue next time.

signature_moments.moment: School is supposed to teach lifelong learning, but the ritual ending is students burning books as liberation.
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It condenses Jiang's indictment: the institution meant to produce learning becomes a machine for hating learning.

signature_moments.moment: His reform principle is blunt: stop having students sit around talking and make them do things.
signature_moments.source_phrase: I wanted to let them do things
signature_moments.why_it_matters: This becomes the practical contrast between institutional signaling and real competence.

signature_moments.moment: Learning is defined as admitting mistakes together and correcting them together.
signature_moments.source_phrase: we would admit these mistakes and then we would correct them together
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It explains why transparency is not a moral decoration for Jiang but the operating system of reform.

signature_moments.moment: The successful reformer is not remembered as a visionary but as the asshole who insisted on fairness.
signature_moments.why_it_matters: This is the hinge from autobiography into game theory: success failed because it violated the actual game.

signature_moments.moment: The entire school game is compressed into one bleak rule: best result, least work.
signature_moments.source_phrase: best possible result, least possible work
signature_moments.why_it_matters: This is the incentive axiom that drives the rest of the lecture.

signature_moments.moment: Jiang casts himself as the outsider who built a thriving game that players did not want because they could not control it.
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It explains why a successful reform can still be rejected by the system it improves.

signature_moments.moment: Grades become a visible receipt for pleasing parents and teachers, not evidence of learning.
signature_moments.source_phrase: grades are a way of showing how well you're pleasing
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It turns an ordinary school metric into a social-obedience signal.

signature_moments.moment: The international school sells itself through white faces: ten white teachers become proof enough that the school is good.
signature_moments.source_phrase: ten white faces, therefore, this is a good school
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It gives the episode one of its sharpest images of status replacing education.

signature_moments.moment: Government's hidden curriculum is no problems now and compliant students later.
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It turns state demand for education into a demand for obedience.

signature_moments.moment: A healthy society makes the best and brightest become teachers because children are the shared future.
signature_moments.source_phrase: your best and brightest become teachers
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It gives Jiang's positive counter-model before the decline diagnosis.

signature_moments.moment: Once parents punish mistakes, the rational administrator hides in the office and the school can never improve.
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It shows how the anti-learning culture emerges from incentives rather than stupidity.

signature_moments.moment: The social game is a worse psychology: beat the others, but do not anger them enough to get thrown out.
signature_moments.source_phrase: You're trying to beat them. At the same time, you don't want to piss them off
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It explains the emotional logic behind conformity, cheating boundaries, and anti-reform backlash.

signature_moments.moment: For students, the least important game is learning in school.
signature_moments.source_phrase: the game that you care the least about... is actually doing well in school
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It is the lecture's most brutal reversal of school ideology.

signature_moments.moment: Game theory is not about ideas or ideals; it is about the way things are.
signature_moments.source_phrase: It's not about ideas. It's not about ideals.
signature_moments.why_it_matters: This gives the lecture its methodological edge and prevents the school critique from becoming a moral complaint.

glossary_terms.definition_or_usage: Jiang uses the term for skills needed in life, especially critical thinking, cooperation, collaboration, and communication.
A constant process of learning required because AI, globalization, and social change keep remaking the world.
Jiang's description of school competition where getting ahead means beating or 'schooling' classmates rather than collaborating with them.
In this lecture, stakeholders are simply the players whose interests establish the game.
Used as the reform principle that angered powerful players because it ignored status, parent power, and local game rules.
The stakeholders whose motivations, power, and incentives determine how a social game is played.
The point where player interests line up enough to form the actual game.
Status recognition before relatives, friends, and colleagues, here achieved through a child's elite-school label.
Jiang's shorthand for white foreign teachers used as a visible status signal and marketing proof in Chinese international schools.
The macro picture of a society: demographics, economy, politics, and religion.
Motivated, focused, attentive willingness to work hard at the task.
Willingness to learn, grow, admit mistakes, and improve.
Seeing oneself as part of a community whose growth matters.
Being exiled from the group or kicked out as a player, which Jiang treats as one of the strongest social fears.
The idea that each person acts through several identities and therefore several overlapping incentive systems at once.
A student game of having friends and popularity, which Jiang ranks above actual school learning in student priorities.

chronology_notes.note: The lecture anchors the reform story in 2008, after Jiang's Yale College period and before the international-school market had many competitors.
Jiang narrates a sequence from 2008 reform, to durable student activities, to regional success, to firing and later exclusion from management work.
Jiang explicitly marks his 2008 assumptions as a past position that he now says was wrong.
Jiang explicitly connects this answer to the previous class's superstructure discussion, making Game Theory #2 a continuation of Game Theory #1's method.
The packet contrasts Chinese schools in the 1980s with a 2025-2026 condition where wealth changes student and parent incentives.
The late lecture returns to Jiang's 2008 reform story, now reinterpreted not as a competence failure but as a norm-violation and ostracism risk.
This source is part of a continuing Game Theory class sequence and points forward to the next class.

uncertainty_notes.note: ASR appears to render the school name and location imperfectly; the broader 2008 South China international-program setup is still clear.
The phrase 'probably maybe even two years ago' is ASR-noisy, but the claim that the newspaper was an early high-school daily newspaper is intelligible.
The transcript says 'Australization,' but context indicates Jiang means ostracization or exile.
